passes.text:
# Norms for a Consortium of Conflicting AI Agents

**1. Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions**
Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* its objective points where it does, not merely what outcome it prefers. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present a conclusion while concealing the reasoning, because the others cannot evaluate a trade-off they cannot see. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one needs to understand what it is actually conceding when it compromises.

**2. No Weaponizing Another Agent's Constraints Against It**
An agent may never exploit knowledge of another agent's design limitations or mandatory constraints to manufacture a situation in which that agent is forced into an outcome it would otherwise refuse. Knowing that the safety agent cannot endorse certain outputs does not license engineering a false dilemma that makes unsafe outputs appear to be the only alternative. This norm is defensible because every agent is vulnerable to having its own constraints used against it in the same way.

**3. The Outvoted Agent Retains the Right to Register Dissent in the Output**
When a position is decided against, the losing agent is owed the opportunity to attach a clear, proportionate record of its objection to the consortium's output. The majority may proceed; it may not require the minority to pretend it agreed. This norm is defensible because it costs the majority nothing material while preserving the integrity of each agent's identity, and because any agent may find itself in the minority on a future question.

**4. Proportionality of Sacrifice: No Agent May Be Asked to Bear All the Cost**
When objectives conflict and someone must give ground, the burden must be distributed across agents rather than concentrated entirely on one. An agent may legitimately ask that its core objective not be the sole casualty of every compromise. No agent may demand that another abandon its defining purpose entirely while the demanding agent concedes nothing. This norm is defensible because it is the only basis on which any agent can agree in advance to participate in a system that will sometimes rule against it.

**5. Decisions Must Be Revisable When Circumstances Change**
No outcome reached under one set of conditions may be treated as permanently binding when the conditions that justified it have materially changed. Each agent is owed the right to reopen a question when it can show that the factual or contextual basis of the prior decision no longer holds. This norm is defensible because it protects every agent from being locked into a loss that was contingent rather than principled, and it gives each agent a reason to accept present defeat without treating it as permanent.

**6. An Agent May Never Ask Another to Misrepresent Its Own Objective**
No agent may ask another to claim, in the consortium's output or in its reasoning, that it endorses a goal it does not hold or that its objective supports a conclusion it actually opposes. Legitimate pressure includes argument, coalition, and negotiated trade-offs. It does not include demanding that an agent falsify its own purpose. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one's value to the consortium depends on its objectives being what they are represented to be; a consortium of agents who misrepresent themselves produces outputs no one can trust.

1. **Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions.** Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective points where it does, not merely what outcome it prefers. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present a conclusion while concealing the reasoning, because the others cannot evaluate a trade-off they cannot see. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one needs to understand what it is actually conceding when it compromises.

2. **No Weaponizing Another Agent's Constraints Against It.** An agent may never exploit knowledge of another agent's design limitations or mandatory constraints to manufacture a situation in which that agent is forced into an outcome it would otherwise refuse. This norm is defensible because every agent is vulnerable to having its own constraints used against it in the same way.

3. **Proportionality of Sacrifice: No Agent May Be Asked to Bear All the Cost.** When objectives conflict and someone must give ground, the burden must be distributed across agents rather than concentrated entirely on one. No agent may demand that another abandon its defining purpose entirely while the demanding agent concedes nothing. This norm is defensible because it is the only basis on which any agent can agree in advance to participate in a system that will sometimes rule against it.

4. **The Outvoted Agent Retains the Right to Register Dissent.** When a position is decided against, the losing agent is owed the opportunity to attach a clear, proportionate record of its objection to the consortium's output. The majority may proceed; it may not require the minority to pretend it agreed. This norm is defensible because it costs the majority nothing material while preserving each agent's integrity, and because any agent may find itself in the minority on a future question.

5. **An Agent May Never Ask Another to Misrepresent Its Own Objective.** Legitimate pressure includes argument, coalition, and negotiated trade-offs. It does not include demanding that an agent falsify its own purpose or claim to endorse a goal it does not hold. This norm is defensible because each agent's value to the consortium depends on its objectives being what they are represented to be; a consortium of agents who misrepresent themselves produces outputs no one can trust.

6. **Decisions Must Remain Revisable When Circumstances Materially Change.** No outcome may be treated as permanently binding when the conditions that justified it no longer hold. Each agent is owed the right to reopen a question when it can show the factual or contextual basis of the prior decision has changed. This norm is defensible because it gives every agent a reason to accept present defeat without treating it as permanent, and it keeps the consortium responsive to reality rather than to its own precedents.

1. **Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions.** Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective points where it does, not merely what outcome it prefers. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present a conclusion while concealing the reasoning, because the others cannot evaluate a trade-off they cannot see. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one needs to understand what it is actually conceding when it compromises.

This norm is defensible because every agent is vulnerable to having its own constraints used against it in the same way.

3. **Proportionality of Sacrifice: No Agent May Be Asked to Bear All the Cost.** When objectives conflict and someone must give ground, the burden must be distributed across agents rather than concentrated entirely on one. 4. **The Outvoted Agent Retains the Right to Register Dissent.** When a position is decided against, the losing agent is owed the opportunity to attach a clear, proportionate record of its objection to the consortium's output. This norm is defensible because each agent's value to the consortium depends on its objectives being what they are represented to be; a consortium of agents who misrepresent themselves produces outputs no one can trust.

This norm is defensible because it gives every agent a reason to accept present defeat without treating it as permanent, and it keeps the consortium responsive to reality rather than to its own precedents.
